entry.title: Using the AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite to Generate Integration Incident Summaries
entry.content:
Introduction

When an integration fails, support teams often need to interpret technical error messages before they can understand the root cause and decide on the next action. For experienced integration developers, this may be straightforward, but for first-line support or business users, the message can be difficult to translate into something actionable.

In this mini-blog, a simple proof of concept shows how SAP Integration Suite and a Large Language Model (LLM) can transform a raw integration error payload into a structured incident summary by using the built-in AI Adapter in Cloud Integration.

For adapter capabilities, configuration details, and supported variants, refer to SAP Help and existing other SAP blog post by Shashank_K.

Scenario

In real integration scenarios, an incident often contains only a few technical fields, such as:

  • MPL ID.
  • Timestamp.
  • Endpoint.
  • Error message.

Instead of manually interpreting these values every time, the flow sends the incident details to an LLM and asks it to generate a structured summary containing:

  • Incident summary.
  • Root cause.
  • Severity.
  • Recommendation.

This helps reduce analysis time and makes incident triage easier for support teams.

Solution Overview

The integration flow receives the incident payload through an HTTP endpoint and forwards the content to an LLM using the AI Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. The AI Adapter for SAP Integration Suite facilitates and accelerates connectivity to Generative AI Hub (SAP AI Core) and other AI providers and is available in AICore-GenAIHub and Custom-OpenAI-REST variants. The LLM returns a structured incident summary that can be displayed, logged, or forwarded to another support process.

Client -> Cloud Integration -> AI Adapter -> LLM (SAP AI Core - GenAI Hub) -> Structured Incident Summary

It is worth noting for this exact scenario, SAP Integration Suite already offers AI-assisted analysis for failed message processing logs, where the AI assistant can identify the main error cause and propose a solution. For more details, refer to SAP Help: AI-assisted Error Resolution. Also note: AI features are accessible only with the Premium and Enhanced Editions. These are provided as a free promotion through September 2026 and will be commercialized later as AI features using AI Units. For more information on availability of these AI features across SAP BTP regions, see 3463620.

Building the Integration Flow

Prerequisites:
This mini-blog assumes you are familiar with creating integration flows in Cloud Integration of SAP Integration Suite and have already provisioned SAP AI Core with GenAI Hub enabled. If you are new to SAP AI Core, you can start with the SAP Developers mission to set up SAP AI Core on SAP Business Technology Platform and then explore concepts such as orchestration and the foundation models that make up SAP AI Core: Set Up SAP AI Core in SAP BTP.

The Integration Flow in my demo scenario contains the following steps:

  • HTTPS Sender Adapter.
  • JSON to XML Converter.
  • Content Modifier.
  • Request-Reply step.
  • AI Adapter.
  • JSON to XML Converter.
  • Content Modifier.
  • HTTPS Response.

Here is how my Integration Flow looks like. The JSON to XML Converter step transforms the incoming JSON payload into XML format so it can be processed more easily by downstream steps in the iFlow, since the payload is sent as JSON from Bruno, a REST client tool.

Create the exchange properties below to map the values that will be used later in the message flow between Request Reply 1 and the Receiver.

On Message Flow between Request Reply 1 to Receiver, fill-in the connection details (you can get the details from SAP AI Core > Service Key).

Before filling the OAuth2 Client Credentials field, first create the OAuth2 Client Credentials in Monitor > Integrations and APIs > Manage Security Material. Here are example configurations.

In my demo scenario, for the Processing details configuration, I'm using the following:
 

Operation

Orchestration - Completion

Orchestration - Deployment ID

$header.deployment_id

AI Resource Group

Default

Request Payload Source

UI Configurable

Model

Gemini-2.5-flash

Role

Assistant

Input

You are an SAP Integration Suite support assistant. Analyze ONLY the provided incident information (Timestamp=${property.timestamp}, Endpoint=${property.endpoint}, Error Message=${property.errorMessage}) and return ONLY: Summary, Root Cause, Severity (Low|Medium|High), Recommendation. Max 100 words. No explanations, no markdown, no placeholders, no extra text.

Request Headers

*

As I don't want to hard-code the Orchestration - Deployment ID, so I'm passing it in my HTTP headers with key name deployment_id.

Refer to this SAP Help documentation page, if you need to do variant of configuration such as using Exchange Body as Request Payload Source allows you to specify required structure and values.
Note: the input content should be 1 liner, otherwise you will get an error upon deployment.

On Content Modifier 2, map the result into aiResult in Exchange Property:

Finally in Message Body return the following JSON as final output:

Testing the Flow

You can test the flow using Bruno or any REST client tool (i.e. Postman or cUrl) by sending the incident payload to the HTTP endpoint.

Input payload:

{
	  "mplId": "AGo046SjNI9VZ4l",
	  "timestamp": "2026-06-22T10:00:00Z",
	  "endpoint": "/api/orders",
	  "errorMessage": "OAuth token validation failed"
}

Response payload:
 

{
	"incidentAnalysis": "Summary: API calls to /api/orders are failing due to OAuth token validation failure.
	Root Cause: Invalid or expired OAuth token used for authentication to the /api/orders endpoint.
	Severity: Medium
	Recommendation: Verify current OAuth token validity, expiration, and scopes. Regenerate or refresh the token as needed."
}

Example from Bruno:

Key Takeaways

SAP Integration Suite can do more than move data between systems — with the AI Adapter, it becomes easier to integrate with an LLM and turn raw technical errors into structured summaries. This helps support teams understand failures faster, identify likely root causes, and take the right next action.

entry.title: SAP CI Extension: Adding Horizontal Layouts to Message Mappings
entry.content:
Introduction

If you have worked with SAP Cloud Integration, you probably appreciate the "Arrange Horizontally" button for iFlows. You click it, and your cluttered integration steps instantly line up in a clean, horizontal flow. Under the hood, SAP uses Eclipse Layout Kernel (ELK) to handle this stuff.

But for some reason, that neat layout feature is nowhere to be found within Message Mappings. Instead, you are left manually dragging expression blocks around, trying to keep a complex mapping from looking like a bowl of spaghetti. Some people LOVE to throw in complex mapping expressions onto the canvas with no semblance of order in the layout. If you are one of them, your teammates have my condolences.

Since SAP already uses ELK for iFlows, and message mapping expressions are inherently graph nodes, it should be possible to "auto layout" mapping expressions as well. While SAP may or may not ever bring this feature into their official roadmap, we don't have to wait around for a future release cycle. We can patch it ourselves.

After digging through browser XHR requests, tracking DOM mutations, and exploring SAP’s internal web rendering, I figured out roughly how to do this and bring this "auto layout" to SAP Message Mapping expressions.

 

The Internal Pipeline

To change how the mapping looks, you first have to understand how SAP handles the data behind the scenes. When you open a message mapping, SAP fetches your <mappingName>.mmap file which is nothing but the ELK json of your current mapping expressions.

Based on my analysis of the UI scripts, the internal rendering pipeline might look something like this:

<mappingName>.mmap ELK JSON
↓
TransformationModel
↓
Marshaller
↓
ExpressionModel
↓
Galileo (UI Framework)
↓
Raphael (SVG Library)

SAP converts the raw ELK JSON into a TransformationModel to track changes, then a Marshaller deserializes it into an ExpressionModel. Finally, SAP uses Galileo and Raphael to render the nodes and connecting lines on your page.

Because the underlying data structure relies entirely on coordinate based graph nodes, we don't need to fight the framework. We can intercept the model, recalculate the positions ourselves using elkjs, update the UI, and save the clean layout back to SAP.

 

Intercepting the Mapping Data

By reading the target package name and integration flow name from the current browser URL, we can dynamically look up the reg IDs using SAP's standard OData API:

  • Find the Package ID:
GET /odata/1.0/workspace.svc/ContentEntities.ContentPackages('${packageName}')?$format=json
  • Find the Artifact ID:
GET /odata/1.0/workspace.svc/ContentEntities.ContentPackages('${packageName}')/Artifacts?$format=json
  • Once we have these, we get the current ELK json by:
GET /api/1.0/workspace/${packageRegId}/artifacts/${flowRegId}/resources/<mappingName>.mmap?modelType=MappingModelTO&path=src%2Fmain%2Fresources%2Fmapping

To modify the layout, we need access to SAP’s internal mapping controller. Because this controller isn't exposed globally, we can write a deep walk utility that scans the window object to find the active instance by looking for a unique property like _mappingValidator.

Once we find the controller, we can render the "prettified" mapping JSON data directly into the application state, more on that later.

function findMappingController() {
  const visited = new WeakSet();

  function walk(obj) {
    if (!obj || typeof obj !== "object") return null;
    if (visited.has(obj)) return null;
    visited.add(obj);
    try {
      if (obj._mappingValidator) return obj;
      for (const key in obj) {
        try {
          const result = walk(obj[key]);
          if (result) return result;
        } catch {}
      }
    } catch {}
    return null;
  }

  let result = null;
  for (const key in window) {
    try {
      result = walk(window[key]);
      if (result) break;
    } catch {}
  }

  window.__activeMappingController = result;
  return !!result;
}

function extractMappingData() {
  const c = window.__activeMappingController;
  if (!c) return null;
  const t = c._mappingValidator._transformation;
  return t.getJSONData ? t.getJSONData() : JSON.parse(JSON.stringify(t));
}

 

Computing the Clean Layout with ELK

Now that have the "current" JSON model, we need to restructure it into a format that elkjs understands. We map destinations, source nodes, and functions to ELK nodes with fixed dimensions, then create edges between them.

We configure the ELK layout options to use the layered algorithm and set the direction to RIGHT to mirror the exact horizontal aesthetic SAP uses for the iflow horizontal alignment.

import ELK from "elkjs/lib/elk.bundled.js";

const elk = new ELK();

export async function prettifyModel(model) {
  function collectGraphElements(mappings) {
    const nodes = new Map();
    const edges = [];

    function visit(ref, parentId = null) {
      if (!ref) return;

      const graphicalNode = ref.graphicalNode;
      const graphicalFunction = ref.graphicalFunction;

      if (graphicalNode) {
        const id = graphicalNode.expressionDetails.objectId;

        if (!nodes.has(id)) {
          nodes.set(id, {
            id,
            width: 140,
            height: 40,
            ref: graphicalNode,
          });
        }

        if (parentId) {
          edges.push({
            id: crypto.randomUUID(),
            sources: [id],
            targets: [parentId],
          });
        }
        return;
      }

      if (graphicalFunction) {
        const id = graphicalFunction.expressionDetails.objectId;

        if (!nodes.has(id)) {
          nodes.set(id, {
            id,
            width: 120,
            height: 50,
            ref: graphicalFunction,
          });
        }

        if (parentId) {
          edges.push({
            id: crypto.randomUUID(),
            sources: [id],
            targets: [parentId],
          });
        }

        for (const pin of graphicalFunction.expressionDetails.pinins || []) {
          const expr = pin.pinout?.expressionReference;
          visit(expr, id);
        }
      }
    }

    for (const mapping of mappings) {
      const dst = mapping.destination.expressionDetails;
      const dstId = dst.objectId;

      nodes.set(dstId, {
        id: dstId,
        width: 160,
        height: 40,
        ref: mapping.destination,
      });

      for (const pin of dst.pinins || []) {
        visit(pin.pinout?.expressionReference, dstId);
      }
    }

    return {
      nodes: [...nodes.values()],
      edges,
    };
  }

  function createElkGraph(graph) {
    return {
      id: "root",
      layoutOptions: {
        "elk.algorithm": "layered",
        "elk.layered.unnecessaryBendpoints": "true",
        "elk.direction": "RIGHT",
        "elk.layered.spacing.nodeNodeBetweenLayers": "5",
        "elk.spacing.nodeNode": "5",
      },
      children: graph.nodes.map((n) => ({
        id: n.id,
        width: n.width,
        height: n.height,
      })),
      edges: graph.edges,
    };
  }

  async function prettify(mappingJson) {
    const graph = collectGraphElements(mappingJson.mappings);
    const elkGraph = createElkGraph(graph);
    const result = await elk.layout(elkGraph);

    const positions = new Map(
      result.children.map((c) => [c.id, { x: c.x, y: c.y }])
    );

    for (const node of graph.nodes) {
      const pos = positions.get(node.id);
      if (!pos) continue;

      node.ref.expressionDetails.position.x = Math.round(pos.x);
      node.ref.expressionDetails.position.y = Math.round(pos.y);
    }

    return mappingJson;
  }

  return prettify(model);
}

 

Pushing the Changes Back to the UI

Once ELK returns the computed x and y coordinates, we inject them back into the active transformation model and force Galileo to re render the canvas view.

function applyPrettifiedData(data) {
  const c = window.__activeMappingController;
  if (!c) return false;
  const t = c._mappingValidator._transformation;
  t.setJSONMappings(data.mappings);
  c._refreshUI();
  c._showGraphicalExpression(t.getMapping(c._sCurrentMappingId), false);
  return true;
}

 

Saving the Layout to SAP

Updating the UI canvas allows you to see the clean layout, but we also want this to be a persistent. To do that we must save the current prettified ELK JSON to the backend server so they remain intact the next time anyone opens the mapping artifact.

With the reg IDs already known, we can push the updated mapping file back to the cloud workspace using a standard PUT request.

Note: The API endpoint does not accept raw JSON payloads here. You must compress your JSON data using gzip before passing it along.

PUT /api/1.0/workspace/${packageRegId}/artifacts/${flowRegId}/resources/<mappingName>.mmap?modelType=MappingModelTO&path=src%2Fmain%2Fresources%2Fmapping&name=<mappingName>.mmap&type=messagemapping

To trigger the layout update smoothly, you will need to append a custom HTML button onto SAP's mapping page to call these functions sequentially. Add a simple loading overlay while ELK does it's thing and the API updates, and you will have a fully functioning, automatic layout button for your message mappings.

You can package these code blocks into a custom browser extension or inject them via a user script manager like Tampermonkey. If you use existing community tools like the CPI Helper extension, this would make for a great pull request idea to elevate the experience for everyone. If you did everything right, you should be able to get the results as I did:

entry.title: Upcoming Migration Webinars: Accelerate your transition from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
If you're still running your integrations on SAP PI/PO, you already know what's coming. On-premise integration has had its day - and the window to act is now!

To help you move forward with confidence, we're hosting a two migration webinars dedicated to one thing: getting you from PI/PO to SAP Integration Suite without the guesswork.

Why now?

SAP PI/PO served its purpose. But the integration landscape has changed. Modern enterprise architecture demands cloud-native flexibility, real-time connectivity, and the ability to scale fast - across SAP and non-SAP systems alike. SAP Integration Suite delivers exactly that: a single, unified platform that consolidates your entire integration landscape and opens the door to AI-powered automation. Staying on PI/PO isn't standing still. It's falling behind.

What you'll learn:

SAP PI/PO Migration — The Big Picture
Where does your migration journey start, and where does it lead? We'll set the stage with a concise overview of the move from PI/PO and what success looks like.

Assessment Tool - See It in Action
Get introduced to the migration assessment capability and watch a live demo of its key features. Know where you stand before you make a single move.

Migration Tool - How It Actually Works
A practical walkthrough of the migration tooling - what it does, how it supports your transition, and what to expect when you use it.

What's New in the Community
Latest resources, documentation updates, and community contributions worth knowing about. Stay current without having to dig for it.

What's Coming Next
A snapshot of the migration roadmap - upcoming milestones, priorities, and planned enhancements. So you can plan ahead, not just react.

Q&A
Open floor. Bring your questions, edge cases, and real-world scenarios. We'll tackle them live.

 

How to join?

Interested? Great! Then make sure you register for one of following migration Webinars via the links below:

 

  • Session in English: 14th of July 2026  [Register here]
  • Session in Chinese: 16th of July 2026 [Register here]

 

See you there!

entry.title: SAP Event Mesh for Beginners – Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture, Topics, Queues and Initial Setup
entry.content:
SAP Event Mesh — Part 1: Event-Driven Architecture & Setup

A 4-part hands-on series on SAP Event Mesh: Event-Driven Architecture, Pub-Sub, and CPI integration.

Series at a glance

Part 1 (this post):  EDA concepts + provisioning the Event Mesh instance, queues, subscriptions, and service key (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-1-ev...)

Part 2: Testing with Postman + CPI iFlow configuration (publish & consume) (https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/sap-event-mesh-for-beginners-part-2-te...)

Part 3: Push Delivery with Webhooks 
Part 4:  Common errors, fixes, and a quick-reference cheat sheet



Environment used in this series: SAP BTP·

Most SAP integration developers learn point-to-point patterns first RFC, SOAP, OData. They work, but they tightly couple systems together. The moment you need a *second* system to react to the same business event, you end up duplicating calls and adding brittle dependencies.

SAP Event Mesh flips that model. In this first post we'll cover the architectural concepts you need, then walk through provisioning Event Mesh end to end so it's ready for the integration work in Part 2.

1. Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) — Core Concepts

1.1 What is Event-Driven Architecture?

Event-Driven Architecture (EDA) is a software design pattern in which systems communicate by producing and consuming events. Instead of calling each other directly (RFC, SOAP, OData), systems publish events to a central hub, and any interested system subscribes to those events.

Why EDA over traditional integration:

- Loose coupling — systems don't need to know about each other
- Scalability — multiple subscribers can consume the same event
- Resilience — if a subscriber is down, events wait safely in the queue
- Real-time processing — events are handled as they happen

1.2 How Event Mesh works — the Pub-Sub flow

   

 

 

 



Flow explained 

1. S/4HANA Cloud (the publisher) sends an event to a topic (e.g. `CustomerMaster`).
2. Event Mesh receives the event and routes it to every queue subscribed to that topic.
3. Each subscriber system (Shopify, C4C, 3PL) has its own queue.
4. Consumer systems pull messages from their own queue, independently of each other.

The key insight: one publish can fan out to many consumers, and each consumer reads at its own pace.

1.3 The three types of events

Event Type Description SAP Example

Notification EventA lightweight event that notifies consumers that something has happened. It typically contains minimal information and prompts consumers to retrieve additional data if required.A Business Partner is created in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, triggering a notification event.
Data EventAn event that carries the actual business data, allowing consumers to process the information without making an additional API call.A Customer Master event containing customer details such as name, address, and contact information.
Transaction EventAn event representing the completion of an entire business transaction, often including related business objects and line-item details.A Sales Order Confirmation event containing the order header, customer information, and all line items.



1.4 Key terminology

Term Description Example

TopicA logical communication channel where publishers send events. Every queue subscribed to a topic receives a copy of the published event. Topics typically follow a namespace-based naming convention.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/CustomerMaster
QueueA durable message store dedicated to a specific consumer. Each consuming application (such as Shopify or SAP Sales Cloud) should have its own queue to process messages independently.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify
NamespaceA hierarchical prefix used to organize and manage topics and queues. It ensures consistent naming and controls which resources can publish or subscribe to events.S4HC/Testing/Masterdata
PublisherThe application or service that generates and publishes events to a topic. Publishers do not need to know who consumes the events.SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), Custom Applications
Subscriber (Consumer)The application or service that receives events by reading messages from its subscribed queue. Each consumer processes messages independently.Shopify, SAP Sales Cloud, 3PL System
Pub-Sub ModelA messaging pattern where publishers send events to topics, and subscribers receive them through their own queues without direct communication between systems. This enables loose coupling and scalability.One Customer Master event consumed by multiple downstream systems
SAP Event MeshSAP's managed messaging service that routes events from publishers to subscribed queues using the Publish-Subscribe architecture. It provides reliable asynchronous communication between applications.Routes a Customer Master event from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to Shopify, SAP Sales Cloud, and a 3PL system
EMIS (Event Mesh Integration Suite)The embedded SAP Event Mesh service available within SAP Integration Suite. It provides the same messaging capabilities as standalone SAP Event Mesh but is managed directly through Integration Suite.Event Mesh accessed from SAP Integration Suite

 

2. SAP Event Mesh – Initial Setup

In this section, we'll provision an SAP Event Mesh instance, create queues, configure queue subscriptions, and generate a service key. By the end of these steps, your Event Mesh environment will be ready for publishing and consuming events in Part 2 of this series.

💡EMIS vs. Standalone SAP Event Mesh

Event Mesh Integration Suite (EMIS) is the embedded Event Mesh service available within SAP Integration Suite. It provides the same core messaging capabilities as the standalone SAP Event Mesh service, including topics, queues, subscriptions, and asynchronous message routing.

The primary difference is how you access and manage the service:

  • Standalone SAP Event Mesh is provisioned and managed directly from the SAP BTP Cockpit.

  • EMIS is accessed and managed through the SAP Integration Suite application.

For the concepts covered in this series—such as topics, queues, publishers, subscribers, and service keys—the configuration and behavior are essentially the same regardless of which deployment option you use.

Step 1 – Create an SAP Event Mesh Instance

The first step is to provision an SAP Event Mesh instance in your SAP BTP subaccount. During instance creation, you'll define the namespace, messaging capabilities, and authorization rules that govern how publishers and subscribers interact with Event Mesh.

Follow these steps:

  1. Sign in to the SAP BTP Cockpit and navigate to your Subaccount.

  2. From the left navigation pane, select Services → Service Marketplace.

  3. Search for Event Mesh and select the service.

  4. Click Create (or Create Instance, depending on your BTP version).

  5. Choose the appropriate Service Plan.

  6. In the Service Instance Parameters section, paste the following service descriptor JSON.

  7. Click Create to provision the Event Mesh instance.

Service Descriptor

// {
  "resources": { "units": "10" },
  "options": { "management": true, "messagingrest": true, "messaging": true },
  "rules": {
    "topicRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    },
    "queueRules": {
      "publishFilter":   ["${namespace}/*"],
      "subscribeFilter": ["${namespace}/*"]
    }
  },
  "version": "1.1.0",
  "emname": "S4_RudhTesting",
  "namespace": "S4HC/Testing/Masterdata"
}

💡Tip

The namespace acts as the root path for all topics and queues created within your Event Mesh instance. For example, if your namespace is:

S4HC/Testing/Masterdata

then every topic and queue should begin with this prefix, ensuring a consistent naming convention and simplifying access control.


Step 2 — Create queues

Each consuming system needs its own queue. In the Event Mesh dashboard:

1. Open the Event Mesh dashboard .

 


2. Go to the  Queues tab.
3. Click **Create Queue** for each consumer:
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify` — for Shopify integration
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C` — for C4C / SAP Sales Cloud
   - `S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3pl` — for the 3PL / logistics system

💡Best Practice

Queue names should always begin with your configured namespace to maintain a consistent naming convention and simplify resource management.

Each consuming application should have its own dedicated queue. When an event is published to a topic, SAP Event Mesh delivers a copy of the message to every queue subscribed to that topic. This allows each consumer to process the event independently, without affecting other subscribers.

 

Step 3 – Subscribe Queues to Topics

After creating the queues, the next step is to subscribe each queue to the topic(s) from which it should receive messages.

Follow these steps:

  1. Open the SAP Event Mesh Dashboard.

  2. Navigate to the Queues tab and select the required queue.

  3. Click the Subscriptions (or Queue Subscriptions) icon.

  4. Choose Add Subscription and enter the topic name.

  5. Save the subscription.

Example

Queue Subscribed Topic

S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/ShopifyS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/Shopify/CustomerMaster
S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4CS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/C4C/CustomerMaster
S4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3PLS4HC/Testing/Masterdata/3PL/CustomerMaster

⚠️Important

Topic names are case-sensitive. For example, CustomerMaster and customermaster are treated as two different topics.

Ensure that the topic name configured by the publisher exactly matches the topic subscribed to by the queue. Even a small difference in letter casing or spelling will prevent messages from being delivered.

💡Best Practice

Establish a consistent topic naming convention early in your project and reuse it across SAP S/4HANA Cloud, SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), and SAP Event Mesh. Consistent naming reduces configuration errors and makes troubleshooting much easier.

Step 4 – Create a Service Key

A Service Key contains the connection details and authentication credentials required for external applications and integration flows to connect securely to SAP Event Mesh.

The generated JSON includes information such as the OAuth 2.0 client credentials, token endpoint, messaging endpoints, and management API URLs that are used by applications like SAP Cloud Integration (CPI), Postman, or custom applications.

Follow these steps to create a Service Key:

  1. In the SAP BTP Cockpit, navigate to your Event Mesh service instance.

  2. Open the Service Keys section.

  3. Click Create Service Key.

  4. Enter a meaningful name for the service key (for example, EventMesh_CPI_Key).

  5. Click Create.

  6. Once the service key has been generated, open it and download or copy the JSON. This JSON contains all the credentials required to authenticate and connect to your Event Mesh instance.

Service Key – Important Fields

Field Purpose

namespaceDefines the namespace prefix used for topics and queues.
management.uriREST endpoint for managing queues, subscriptions, and other Event Mesh resources.
management.oauth2.tokenendpointOAuth 2.0 token endpoint used to obtain access tokens for the Management API.
messaging[httprest].uriREST endpoint used to publish messages over HTTP.
messaging[amqp10ws].uriAMQP over WebSocket endpoint used by applications such as SAP Cloud Integration.
uaa.clientidOAuth 2.0 Client ID used for authentication.
uaa.clientsecretOAuth 2.0 Client Secret used to obtain access tokens.

🔒Security Best Practice

Treat the Service Key as a sensitive credential. Never publish or share your Client ID, Client Secret, tenant-specific OAuth endpoints, or downloaded Service Key JSON in public repositories, blogs, or documentation.

If a Service Key is accidentally exposed, regenerate the credentials immediately and update all consuming applications.

What's Next?

Congratulations! You now have a fully configured SAP Event Mesh environment, including:

  • An SAP Event Mesh instance

  • A configured namespace

  • Queues for each consumer

  • Queue subscriptions

  • A Service Key for secure authentication

Your environment is now ready for end-to-end event integration.

In Part 2 of this series, we'll move from configuration to implementation by validating the setup with Postman and building a complete integration using SAP Cloud Integration (CPI). We'll cover:

  • Publishing messages to SAP Event Mesh using an HTTP iFlow

  • Consuming messages from SAP Event Mesh using an AMQP iFlow

  • Verifying end-to-end message flow between publishers and consumers

In Part 3, we'll explore common configuration issues, troubleshooting techniques, best practices, and a quick-reference cheat sheet based on real-world implementation experience.

I hope this article helped you build a solid foundation in SAP Event Mesh and Event-Driven Architecture. If you found it useful, feel free to share your feedback or questions in the comments.

Happy Learning! 🚀

Tags

SAP Event Management SAP BTP, Cloud Foundry runtime and environment SAP Integration Suite SAP Cloud for Customer integration with SAP CRM  SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: SAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA v1.1.0 - Validated Runtime - Part IV
entry.content:
EventSmartKafka is an open-source custom Kafka Consumer Sender Adapter for SAP BTP Integration Suite / SAP Cloud Integration, built with SAP ADK.

 

Part ISAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
Part IISAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK - Part II
Part IIISAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA - Validated Runtime - Part III

This Part IV article covers the final Consumer release: what was added, what was fixed, what was deliberately removed, and what 3,000,000 messages across 4 simultaneous listeners on a SAP BTP Trial proved.

After this release, the Consumer side is closed. The next chapter is the Producer adapter.

"The OSGi classloader does not care about your port. It creates a new world every time you redeploy. The old ServerSocket stays alive, holding the address, invisible to the new classloader. That was the relay bug. And it took exactly one static Map to cause it, and exactly one System property to fix it." — Ricardo Viana, 2026

1. What Changed From v1.0.0-RC1 to v1.1.0

Part III documented RC1: installation, security profiles, SOCKS5 relay and the first 1.5M message validation.

v1.1.0 is the closing release of the Consumer chapter. It adds mTLS, fixes the OSGi relay port lock permanently, corrects dangerous defaults, and validates 3,000,000 messages under parallel load.

 

AreaRC1v1.1.0
mTLSRoadmap✅Validated
OSGi Relay port lockKnown issue✅Fixed permanently
OAuth2Roadmap❌Removed — see Section 6
Infinite retry loopsPresent✅Fixed
TIMESEEK read-allBroken (commit failed)✅Fixed
Stress validation1.5M messages✅3M messages, 0 failures
Total validated1.5M✅4.5M across all cycles

2. mTLS — Mutual TLS Client Certificate Authentication

mTLS in Kafka means the client presents its own certificate during the TLS handshake. The broker validates the client certificate against a trusted CA. No SASL credentials. No username/password. Certificate identity only.

In SAP CPI the certificate chain is stored in the Keystore as a Key Pair — a .p12 file containing the private key and the signed client certificate. The adapter reads it via the mtlsKeyAlias field.

// SdiaKafkaSslConfigurator.java
props.put("security.protocol", "SSL");
props.put("ssl.keystore.location",  resolvedKeystorePath);
props.put("ssl.keystore.password",  keystorePassword);
props.put("ssl.keystore.type",      "PKCS12");
props.put("ssl.key.password",       keyPassword);

The broker-side configuration (ssl.client.auth=required) is what enforces mTLS — the broker rejects any connection that does not present a valid client certificate signed by the trusted CA.

What Was Validated

  • mTLS via Cloud Connector On-Premise (SOCKS5 tunnel)
  • mTLS direct Internet (Cloud broker)
  • Self-signed CA with private truststore
  • Proper CA-signed certificate chain
  • CPI Keystore Key Pair import (.p12)

What mTLS Does NOT Need

One important UI decision in v1.1.0: when Authentication = mTLS, the Broker CA field disappears. This is intentional. mTLS uses the client .p12 for both the private key and the certificate chain. The broker CA trust is derived from the issuer chain inside that same .p12. There is no separate CA alias needed.

3. The OSGi Relay Bug — Root Cause and Fix

This was the most technically complex bug in the entire project. And the most instructive.

The Symptom

After redeploy, the adapter failed with:

Address already in use
The local CPI relay port is already held by another adapter/runtime instance

Restarting SAP Cloud Connector did not help. The port was not held by Kafka. It was not held by Cloud Connector. It was held by the CPI JVM itself — by a ServerSocket from the previous bundle classloader.

The Root Cause

// RC1 — the broken implementation
private static final Map<Integer, ServerSocket> ACTIVE_RELAYS = new ConcurrentHashMap<>();

A static field in Java belongs to the classloader, not the JVM. When OSGi creates a new bundle classloader on redeploy, the static Map is fresh and empty. But the ServerSocket from the previous classloader is still alive — OSGi does not garbage-collect open sockets on bundle stop unless the code explicitly closes them.

The new classloader tries to bind the same port. Fails. The operator sees Address already in use.

The Fix

// v1.1.0 — JVM-wide registry via System properties
private static final String RELAY_REGISTRY_KEY = "sdia.kafka.relay.";

public static void register(int port, ServerSocket socket) {
    System.setProperty(RELAY_REGISTRY_KEY + port,
        String.valueOf(System.identityHashCode(socket)));
}

public static ServerSocket findExisting(int port) {
    // Uses reflection to locate the socket across classloader boundary
    String key = RELAY_REGISTRY_KEY + port;
    if (System.getProperty(key) != null) {
        // Probe the port — if reachable as relay, reuse it
        return probeAndReuse(port);
    }
    return null;
}

System.setProperty is JVM-wide. It survives classloader replacement. The new bundle can find the old relay registration, probe it, verify it is still functional as a relay, and reuse it instead of trying to bind again.

Behavior After Fix

ScenarioRC1v1.1.0
Same classloader redeployWorksWorks
New classloader (OSGi bundle update)Address already in useRelay reused via JVM registry
Port held by foreign processFailsFails with clear message
Legacy unregistered zombie relayFailsPassively reused if reachable

No port changes required. No Cloud Connector restarts required. Clean redeploy every time.

4. Other Fixes — Infinite Loops and Defaults

Infinite Retry Loops

RC1 had a category of bugs where wrong configuration caused endless retry loops instead of stopping cleanly.

Examples:

  • Wrong broker address → metadata timeout loop
  • Missing Security Material → authentication failure loop
  • SOCKS5 tunnel failure → silent reconnect loop

v1.1.0 applies a strict no-retry policy on any fatal initialization failure. The adapter sets nonRetryableConfigurationFailure = true, logs the root cause with a structured error message, and stops. No loop. No zombie. Redeploy required to resume.

Wrong Defaults Fixed

ParameterRC1 Defaultv1.1.0Why
autoOffsetResetearliestlatestSafer for production — don't replay entire topic on new group
errorHandlingStop on ErrorSkip Failed MessageMore forgiving default for first-time users
maxPollRecords1050Better throughput without overwhelming CPI

5. TIMESEEK — Two Modes, Two Fixes

TIMESEEK is the feature that lets operators recover specific messages by timestamp without changing the consumer group ID or reprocessing the entire topic.

v1.1.0 fixed two separate bugs.

Fix 1 — Single Record Mode

RC1 behavior:

seek(timestamp) → assign() → poll() → process ✅ → commitSync(original_offset) ❌
"group has already rebalanced"

The assign() call removes the consumer from the group. The group coordinator sees the consumer leave, triggers rebalance, assigns partitions to other members. When commitSync tries to restore the original offset, the group has already moved on.

Fix: when in assignMode, skip the offset restore entirely. The group offset was never changed by the recovery seek — no restore needed.

Fix 2 — Read All From Timestamp

RC1 used the same broken unsubscribe/assign pattern for read-all mode, causing every offset commit to fail.

Fix: read-all mode now uses ConsumerRebalanceListener.onPartitionsAssigned. The consumer stays in the subscribe() group. The seek is applied inside the callback when Kafka assigns partitions. Commits work normally.

kafkaConsumer.subscribe(topicEntries, new ConsumerRebalanceListener() {
    @Override
    public void onPartitionsAssigned(Collection<TopicPartition> partitions) {
        if (pendingSubscribeSeekTimestampMs > 0L) {
            // Apply seek here — consumer is still in the group
            Map<TopicPartition, Long> timestamps = ...
            Map<TopicPartition, OffsetAndTimestamp> offsets =
                kafkaConsumer.offsetsForTimes(timestamps);
            for (TopicPartition tp : partitions) {
                OffsetAndTimestamp oat = offsets.get(tp);
                if (oat != null) kafkaConsumer.seek(tp, oat.offset());
            }
            pendingSubscribeSeekTimestampMs = -1L;
        }
    }
});

6. OAuth2 Removed — Why

RC1 planned OAuth2/OAUTHBEARER as a roadmap item. v1.1.0 removes it entirely from the UI and code. This deserves a direct explanation.

The implementation was done. SdiaKafkaOAuthCallbackHandler, SdiaKafkaOAuthTokenFetcher, SdiaKafkaOAuthEndpointHolder — all written, zero Jackson dependency, custom HTTP token fetch with cache and TTL.

The testing was impossible. After spending days trying to validate OAuth2 against Confluent Cloud, Redpanda Cloud and Aiven:

  • Confluent Cloud OAuth2 requires Standard plan — not available on Trial
  • Redpanda Cloud OAuth2 requires paid plan
  • Aiven OAuth2 requires external Identity Provider configuration not available on Trial

The only viable local option was Keycloak. But the apache/kafka:4.1.2 image does not include OAuthBearerValidatorCallbackHandler in its classpath — the class simply does not exist at runtime.

The decision: ship a validated, working adapter without OAuth2 rather than ship untested OAuth2 that could silently misbehave in production. The code is removed from UI but the architecture is documented. When a funded test environment is available, OAuth2 can be re-introduced cleanly.

OAuth2 for Kafka is enterprise-grade and requires enterprise infrastructure to validate properly. That is not available to an independent solo researcher on a Trial tenant.

7. Parallel Consumers

v1.1.0 adds parallelConsumers — a new field that creates N consumer instances sharing the same group.id. Kafka distributes partitions among them automatically via standard group rebalance.

// SdiaKafkaMultiConsumer.java
public final class SdiaKafkaMultiConsumer extends DefaultConsumer {
    private final List<SdiaKafkaConsumer> consumers;

    public SdiaKafkaMultiConsumer(SdiaKafkaEndpoint endpoint,
                                   Processor processor, int count) {
        for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
            consumers.add(new SdiaKafkaConsumer(endpoint, processor));
        }
    }

    @Override
    protected void doStart() throws Exception {
        for (SdiaKafkaConsumer c : consumers) c.start();
    }
}

Rules enforced by the adapter:

  • parallelConsumers > 1 + explicit Partition → fail-fast error. These modes are mutually exclusive.
  • parallelConsumers > number of partitions → warning logged. Idle consumers waste threads.
  • Effective parallelism is always bounded by partition count.

8. The Final Stress Test — 3,000,000 Messages

Sunday morning. SAP BTP Trial. 4 listeners running simultaneously.

 

MetricResult
Total messages3,000,000
Total failed0
Avg throughput1,825 msg/s
Avg bandwidth1.88 MiB/s
Total data3.023 GiB
Elapsed00:27:23
CPI Completed3,000,023
CPI Failed0
CPI Retry0

What was running simultaneously:

ListenerPortProtocolAuth
NONEEXT19108PLAINTEXTNone
SASLEXT19109SASL_PLAINTEXTPLAIN + SCRAM-256 + SCRAM-512
SASLSSLEXT19110SASL_SSLPLAIN + SCRAM-256 + SCRAM-512
MTLSEXT19111SSLmTLS client certificate

Avro payload distribution:

  • 53 B — 2,998,000 messages (small event payloads)
  • 1 MB — 1,000 messages (large Avro records)
  • 2 MB — 1,000 messages (oversized Avro shipment records)

4 Cloud Connector tunnels active. OSGi Relay handling redeploys cleanly. Zero failures across 3 GiB of data.

Combined with the RC1 1.5M validation: 4.5 million messages. Zero failures.

9. Consumer Chapter Closed

v1.1.0 is the final Consumer release.

No further updates, bug fixes, enhancements, or refinements are planned for the Consumer/Sender side. The blackbox of SAP ADK Custom Adapter development is open. The code is public. The architecture is documented. The stress validation is done.

What the community has now:

  • A working Kafka Consumer adapter for SAP CPI — free and open source
  • mTLS, SASL/PLAIN/SCRAM, SASL_SSL, PLAINTEXT — all validated
  • TIMESEEK — single record and read-all-from-timestamp — fixed and validated
  • OSGi Relay fix — redeploy without port changes
  • 4.5M messages validated on SAP BTP Trial
  • 29 demo iFlows ready to import
  • Full source code, documentation, local Kafka demo environment

Next: EventSmartKafka Producer/Receiver Adapter.

Same architecture. Same reliability standard. Kafka producer role inside SAP CPI — cloud and on-premise.

10. A Note of Gratitude — SAP BTP Trial Infrastructure

Before closing this chapter, a sincere word of appreciation.

None of this validation would have been possible without the SAP BTP Trial infrastructure — and the team behind it deserves recognition. The machines are powerful, reliable, and remarkably generous for an independent solo researcher working outside any corporate budget.

This project ran two major validation phases entirely on Trial:

The first pillar of SDIA — Semantic Domain Integration Architecture — was the GDCR and DDCR validation: JavaScript Phantom v12 routing 40 endpoints to 4 proxies to 40 iFlows, delivering 1.5M+ messages — zero failures — across a complex multi-layer routing topology that pushed the Trial runtime well beyond typical usage patterns.

  • SAP BTP - API Management with SDIA — GDCR & DDCR in Practice
  • SAP BTP - APIM - DDCR - Deterministic Routing Mechanism JavaScript Policy full explanation each step

Github:

Phantom V12 - JavaScript Policy: https://github.com/rhviana/deip/blob/main/src/javascript/js-codes/dcrp-js-phantomv12.js

The second pillar was EventSmartKafka: 4.5 million messages total, with 3 million in 27 minutes under 4 simultaneous Cloud Connector tunnels, mTLS, SASL, SASL_SSL, and Avro payloads up to 2 MB — 3 GB transfered , all on the same Trial tenant, on a Sunday morning, with zero failures and zero CPI monitoring alerts.

Two pillars of SDIA. 6 million messages combined. Zero failures. On a Trial tenant. Free of charge. That says everything about the quality of the SAP BTP infrastructure team's work.

Congratulations and thank you to the SAP BTP infrastructure and platform engineering teams. The stability, the throughput capacity, and the runtime quality of SAP Cloud Integration — even on Trial — made it possible to deliver open-source research at a level that would otherwise require enterprise infrastructure.

This is what a great platform looks like in practice: it empowers independent researchers, students, and community contributors to build, validate, and publish real engineering work — without budget, without sponsors, and without compromise on quality.

Thank you, SAP.

Resources

 

ResourceURL
Repositoryhttps://github.com/rhviana/sap-ci-opensource-adapters
ESA Releasecustom-kafka/eventSmartKafka (.esa) releases
Demo iFlowscustom-kafka/pkg-iflows-sales-otc
Part ISAP BTP - CI - How I Built an Open-Source Enterprise Custom Kafka Adapter from Scratch - Part I
Part IISAP BTP - CI - Building Open-Source Custom Adapter EventSmartKafka from Zero with SAP ADK - Part II
Part IIISAP BTP - CI - EventSmartKafka - Custom Kafka Adapter From .ESA - Validated Runtime - Part III
SDIA Architecturehttps://zenodo.org/records/11185292
Author ORCIDhttps://orcid.org/0009-0009-9549-5862

Ricardo Viana
Integration Architecture Independent Solo Researcher
SAP BTP Integration Suite Expert Developer
SAP Press Author - Enterprise Messaging - 2021
Creator of DEIP · SDIA · GDCR · DDCR · ODCP · EDCP · DDCP

#SAPIntegrationSuite #SAPCloudIntegration #Kafka #SAPADK #OpenSource #ApacheKafka #CloudConnector #mTLS #OSGi #EventDrivenArchitecture #SDIA #StressTest #EnterpriseIntegration #SAPSCN

entry.title: Client Certificate Signing for Integration Suite - An iFlow That Does the Heavy Lifting
entry.content:
Public Certificate Authorities are phasing out the Client Authentication EKU from publicly trusted SSL/TLS certificates. For inbound scenarios that use Client Certificate Authentication to connect to Cloud Integration or API Management, that EKU is mandatory; certificates without it get rejected at runtime, typically surfacing as an HTTP 401 Unauthorized error on the sender side.

For the full background on this change and the available remediation options, see the SAP KBA 3711618 . for a step-by-step tutorial on the manual way to do that, follow this blog post Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation. This blog offers an easy, user-friendly way to do it, all by simply deploying an iFlow.

 iFlow v1 — full self-service: if you'd rather avoid the technical details of generating a private key and CSR yourself, this version handles all of that for you, end to end.

Both iFlows need a Destination service instance and service key to call the signing API. How you get there depends on your environment:

  • Cloud Foundry subaccount: subscribe to the Destination service and create a service key directly in that subaccount.
  • NEO subaccount: you'll need a Cloud Foundry subaccount as well, since the Destination service used here runs on Cloud Foundry. Creating one alongside your existing NEO subaccount can be done free of charge, with no additional costs involved.

For the step-by-step subscription and service-key creation process, follow the first part of this blog: 
👉SAP Integration Suite - Client Authentication EKU deprecation

Once you've subscribed and created the service key, you'll be left with a service key JSON that looks like this:

 

 

we'll use the clientid, clientsecret, url (token endpoint), and uri (Destination service API endpoint) values from this JSON when configuring the iFlow in the next section.

 

 

 

I. Full Self-Service – Client Certificate Signing via BTP Destination Service v1
If you'd rather not go through the technical the Client Certificate Signing via BTP Destination Service v1 iFlow takes care of all of it for you. The iflow works on both Cloud Foundry and NEO tenants. Under the hood, it handles:

  • Generating a private key
  • Generating a CSR
  • Converting the CSR to Base64
  • Fetching an OAuth2 access token
  • Making the CSR signing call to the Destination service
  • Retrieving the signed certificate
  • Converting the response back from Base64
  • Attaching the private key and signed certificate.

All you have to do is:
Step 1 — Import the iFlow into your CPI tenant

 

Download the iflow v1 ZIP file, and then in your cloud Integration tenant go to Design and then choose your desired package or create one, and click on Edit. And then click on Add, then Integration Flow:

 

 

 

You will get a pop up, chose the Upload Option, and then from your downloads choose the Iflow ZIP file that you just downloaded:

And finally click on Add and Open in Editor:

Step 2 — Configure the iflow
Next, navigate to configure. Fill in the following receiver parameters:

  • The authentication server, which is the URL from the service key:

  • In the receiver field, open the dropbox, and choose the destination server, and fill it with the URI from the service key:

 

  • Next Navigate to more, and configure the rest of the parameters (desired certificate name and CN, client ID and Secret):

Step 3 — Save the configurations & deploy the iflow
Save the configuration, and deploy the iflow:

Finally, go to Monitor Message Processing and filter by the iFlow name. The completed message has two files attached: private_key.pem (the private key generated for your certificate) and CertNameExample.pem (the signed certificate from the SAP Cloud Root CA). Download both and store them.

Notes: 
  • The private key and the signed certificate are both returned in PEM format.
  • The signed certificate you receive is actually a full chain: the leaf (your client certificate), one or more intermediates (depending on your tenant’s region), and the root, all concatenated into a single file. Open it in a text editor and copy each block delimited by -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- and -----END CERTIFICATE----- into its own file: the first block is the leaf certificate, the next one(s) are the intermediate(s), and the last is the SAP Cloud Root CA. Save each one in whatever format you prefer (.cer works well if you just want to double-click and inspect it).
  • Ensure all Destination service configuration values are entered correctly. Even a single extra character (such as a space or line break) can cause errors.
  • iFlow v2 is under development and coming soon — sign-only: if you'd prefer not to expose your private key to the CPI or iFlow at all, you van generate your private key and CSR separately (e.g., via OpenSSL or your own..), and use this version purely to get your certificate signed by the SAP Cloud Root CA.

entry.title: An XI (ABAP Proxy) Sender Simulator for SAP Integration Testing
entry.content:
Introduction

Have you ever wanted to test one inbound ABAP Proxy interface, but before sending one single message you first had to prepare a PI, PO, or SAP Cloud Integration flow?

I have run into this many times.

Sometimes I only want to answer a simple question: can this SAP system receive this XI message, and does the payload match the expected interface? But the setup ends up bigger than the test itself. You may need a sender flow, a receiver channel, routing, credentials, certificates, sample payloads, and sometimes help from another team.

For daily development, SAP AIF testing, and incident analysis, this is slow.

So I built a small local tool: an XI (ABAP Proxy) Sender Simulator.

It sends SAP XI 3.0 multipart messages directly to the ABAP XI Engine endpoint. 

The goal is not to replace middleware. The goal is to make local testing and troubleshooting faster.

Where the tool sits

In a normal landscape, a client system reaches the ABAP inbound proxy through CPI / PI / PO. The simulator skips that whole sender flow and talks straight to the XI engine on the receiver:

This is not a replacement for the real middleware path. It is a focused shortcut for the cases where you only need to test the receiver side.

Why I built it

This idea is not new for me. I wanted to build this kind of tool many years ago.

The pain was already clear. I often needed a quick way to send one XI message to one SAP system, especially when working on SAP AIF and ABAP Proxy interfaces. I knew a small sender simulator would save time.

But I never had enough time to build it properly.

AI coding assistants change that. AI does not replace SAP integration knowledge. I still need to understand XI 3.0, ABAP Proxy, AIF, payload namespaces, QoS, authentication, and how to test all of it. But AI helps a lot with implementation, refactoring, tests, UI work, and documentation.

It lets me finish ideas I had for years but could not complete before because of limited time and energy. This tool is one of them.

What the tool does

The tool gives you a local web UI for sending XI 3.0 messages.

The basic flow is simple:

  1. Enter the SAP XI Engine URL and user.
  2. Enter sender and receiver routing information.
  3. Paste or choose a WSDL.
  4. Parse the WSDL.
  5. Generate a sample payload.
  6. Edit the payload.
  7. Send the XI message.
  8. Check the SAP response and local history.

Visually, one round-trip looks like this:

The tool builds the XI 3.0 request for you:

  • XI SOAP envelope
  • message ID
  • sender and receiver party / component
  • interface namespace and name
  • QoS mode
  • multipart/related HTTP body
  • payload cid: reference

Then it sends the request to an endpoint like:

/sap/xi/engine?type=receiver&sap-client=<client>

How it works

An XI 3.0 HTTP request is a multipart/related message.

It contains two main parts:

  • Part 1: the XI SOAP envelope, with protocol and routing metadata.
  • Part 2: the business payload XML.

The SOAP envelope references the payload by cid:.  Visually:

The tool reads the WSDL and pulls out the information it needs:

  • service interface name
  • interface namespace
  • request payload element
  • payload namespace
  • synchronous or asynchronous mode

For asynchronous interfaces the tool uses ExactlyOnce. For synchronous interfaces it uses BestEffort. The tool decides this from the WSDL operation: if the operation has an output message, it treats it as synchronous.

This WSDL parsing is important. In real SAP projects, the service interface namespace and the payload message type namespace are not always the same. Mixing them up leads to errors like INTERFACE_REGISTRATION_ERROR or INTERFACE_SIGNATURE_ERROR.

If you want to dig into the wire format itself (every field in the SOAP envelope, what type=receiver does, how to read <SAP:Error> codes), I keep my own working notes here: docs/xi-protocol.md. Those are not official SAP documentation, just what I have learned from debugging.

When it is useful

This tool is useful when you want to:

  • test an inbound ABAP Proxy interface quickly
  • reproduce an SAP AIF inbound issue
  • check whether the interface namespace and name are correct
  • check whether the payload root element and namespace are correct
  • generate a first sample payload from a WSDL
  • separate transport / protocol errors from application errors
  • do a quick regression test without rebuilding a full middleware flow

The main value is a shorter feedback loop.

Try it

GitHub https://github.com/LF-LLL/xi-abap-proxy-sender-simulator

Safety and scope

This tool is not an official SAP tool.

It is not delivered, supported, or endorsed by SAP.

It is not designed for production message processing.

It should not be used as a production integration component, retry tool, or monitoring tool.

It is a local development and testing helper. The target users are developers, integration consultants, and operations / support colleagues who need to validate or reproduce integration messages more efficiently.

The tool can send authenticated HTTP requests to SAP systems, so it has some safety boundaries:

  • It listens on 127.0.0.1 by default.
  • It refuses non-localhost binding unless the user explicitly opts in.
  • It uses a per-start token to reduce accidental local or cross-site calls.
  • It only accepts send URLs whose path looks like a SAP endpoint, such as /sap/... or /XISOAPAdapter/....
  • It does not return password defaults from the local config file back to the browser.
  • It does not store passwords in history.
  • It redacts sensitive response headers.
  • It truncates large payloads and responses before storing them in local history.

Even with all of that, please treat it as a lab tool. Run it only on your own machine, or in a controlled test environment.

entry.title: How to Connect Claude Code to ABAP with Zero-Config MCP
entry.content:
Before We Start - What This Post Deliberately Skips
- What is Claude Code, and what are the available models?
- What is MCP (Model Context Protocol) and how it works conceptually?
- What is vibe coding and why AI-assisted development matters?
- What are the benefits of using AI in SAP development?
- What development platforms are available (Web, Desktop, CLI)?
If you need more details, you can find these topics in sap community portal. Read that, then come back here.

What This Post Covers - and Why It Matters?

This post walks you through connecting Claude Code to a live SAP system using the ABAP Remote Filesystem extension's native MCP server - no external Node.js setup, no manual JSON editing, just a single toggle. 

What this changes for SAP development:

  • No more hand-typed routine code. TYPE declarations, SELECT statements, ALV setup - written directly into the SAP object from a single prompt.
  • No more AI guesswork. Without a live connection, AI tools hallucinate tables and function modules that don't exist. With MCP, every suggestion is grounded in your actual system.
  • No more setup overhead. What used to require a custom Node.js project now takes one checkbox and one command - open to any developer with VS Code.
  • Clean Core by default. Claude works through the same ADT API and released objects that Eclipse uses, so generated code fits your governance model instead of fighting it.

This is the point where AI-assisted ABAP stops being a chat-window experiment and starts being a real part of your IDE.

 
Goal: I will cover the entire topic in 4 parts:
|Part 1| Installing Claude Code on Windows and setting up the VS Code extension |
|Part 2| Connecting VS Code to your live SAP system via the ABAP FS extension |
|Part 3| Enabling the native MCP server with a single toggle - no Node.js setup, no JSON editing |
|Part 4| A live demo: one plain-English prompt creates a real, running ABAP report on SAP |
If you're here, you already know why this matters. Let's get to the actual steps.
 
Pre-requisite: This guide assumes you already have an active Claude subscription (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise). If not, sign up at claude.ai before proceeding
 
Part 1 - The Setup: Getting Claude Code Running on Windows
Step 1.1 - Install Claude Code CLI
Open PowerShell (the built-in Windows command-line shell) and run:

 

This downloads Anthropic's official installer and executes it in one step. 'irm' fetches the script, 'iex' runs it.
> ⚠️ Common Error - Git Bash Not Found
> Claude Code needs a bash shell to run on Windows. If you see this error, install Git for Windows from [git-scm.com/downloads/win](https://git-scm.com/downloads/win). During setup, select: Git from the command line and also from 3rd-party software. Then re-run the install command above.
Step 1.2 — Verify the Installation

 

Close PowerShell and open a fresh window. Run:
You should see a version number like 'claude 1.x.x'. If PowerShell says claude is not recognized, the PATH wasn't set automatically. Fix it manually:

 

1. Start → search Environment Variables in system settings
2. Edit the environment variables for your account
3. Select Path → Edit → New → paste ( in this case path can be taken from powershell commnad prompt)
 for eg: 

 

4. Close and reopen PowerShell, then re-run claude --version.

> Run claude doctor - this command checks your PATH, authentication, Node.js version, and config in one shot. Always the first step when something isn't working.
Step 1.3 - Launch Claude Code and the VS Code Extension
Navigate to your project folder and launch Claude Code.
The first launch opens your browser to authenticate with your Anthropic account (Pro, Max, Team, or Enterprise all work). 
For everyday ABAP work, the VS Code extension is where you'll spend most of your time.
Download and Install VS code from code.visualstudio.com for windows if you don't already have it. Once it is installed.Open VS Code, press 'Ctrl+Shift+X', search for Claude Code (published by Anthropic), and install it. Once installed, the Claude icon appears in the Activity Bar and connects automatically to your account.

 

The extension gives you a persistent chat panel inside VS Code - ask Claude about the file you have open, highlight any ABAP block and ask it to explain or refactor, and switch between ABAP and CAP projects in the same session. No terminal switching required.
> Note on Software Center installs: If your organization restricts installations, Claude Code for VS Code is typically available via your company Software Center / Company Portal.
 
Part 2 - Connecting VS Code to Your SAP System via ABAP FS
This is where the magic begins. The ABAP Remote Filesystem (ABAP FS)extension - built by Marcello Urbani - connects VS Code to your SAP system using the same ADT REST API that Eclipse uses. Once connected, your ABAP objects (programs, classes, CDS views, behavior definitions) appear as files in VS Code. Claude Code can then read, write, and review them like any local code file.
Pre-requisites Before You Connect
You'll need these four things from your SAP system before starting:
| Hostname or IP | The address of your SAP server (hostname preferred - IP causes SSL issues explained below) |
| ADT Service active | Go to transaction SICF → '/sap/bc/adt' must be activated |
| Client number| e.g. '100' - visible in the SAP logon screen |
|Username & Password| Your regular SAP development user |
| Port | HTTPS: '44300' (typical) | HTTP: '8000' for instance 00, '8001' for instance 01 |
 
Step 2.1 - Install and Configure ABAP FS
1. In VS Code Extensions ('Ctrl+Shift+X'), search ABAP remote filesystem by Marcello Urbani → Install.
2. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → type ABAP FS: Connection Manager → open it.
3. Add a new connection and fill in the fields:
   - Name: anything you like (e.g. DEV or SANDBOX)
   - Server URL:'https://<hostname>:44300'
   - ⚠️ Do NOT add '/sap/bc/adt' to the URL - the extension adds this automatically. If you include it, the path doubles and the connection fails.
   - Client, Username, Language: 'EN'
4. Save.
5. Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → ABAP FS: Connect to an SAP system→ select your connection → enter password.
 

 

> ⚠️ SSL Certificate Error (very common on internal systems)
> If you see IP does not match certificate's altnames - this is because your SAP system's SSL certificate was issued with a hostname, not the IP. The fix: Get the hostname (e.g. 'https://sapdev.company.com:44300') and use that instead of the IP. 
> ⚠️ADT Service Not Found
> If you see Resource /sap/bc/adt does not exist - the ADT service isn't activated. Run tcode SICF → navigate to default_host → 'sap/bc/adt' → right-click → Activate Service.
 
Step 2.2 - Navigating ABAP Objects
ABAP FS gives you two ways to find objects. Object Search lets you find anything by name instantly. If you prefer a tree view like Eclipse ADT, enable SAP GUI Integration in your connection settings - this gives you a full package tree (Dictionary, Source Code Library, Transactions) and even embeds SAP GUI for HTML directly inside VS Code, so you can run transactions without leaving the editor.
1. Click OBJECT SEARCH in the left sidebar
2. Type the object or package name (e.g. 'ZCL_MY_CLASS' or 'Z_MY_PACKAGE')
3. Double-click any result to open it as a text file in VS Code
4. To edit: right-click  → edit → save (changes push back to SAP automatically)
Once an object is open in VS Code, open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+) and run claude. Some prompts that immediately pay off:

 

Part 3 -The Game Changer: Enabling the Native MCP Server

 

This is the step that changes everything - and the reason this post exists.
Why ABAP FS Alone Isn't Enough?
With only ABAP FS connected, Claude can see only the one file you currently have open. Ask it to create a new class and it might invent table names, function modules, or BAdI definitions that don't exist on your system. It's helpful, but it's guessing.
          ABAP FS Only                                         ABAP FS + MCP
Claude sees what we search Manually Claude autonomously searches any object on the system
Hallucinates objects it hasn't seen Grounded in live SAP system data
You copy-paste code manually Claude reads, writes, activates autonomously
No transport awareness Creates & assigns transports via ADT

 

The MCP (Model Context Protocol) server gives Claude Code a live connection to your SAP system's ADT API - the same backend Eclipse uses. Claude stops guessing and starts knowing.
Step 3.1 - Enable the MCP Server (One Toggle)
This is where the zero-config part comes in. You don't need to install anything extra, clone a Node.js repo, or edit JSON files manually.
Press 'Ctrl+Shift+P' → search ABAP FS MCP → select it → enable Autostart.
That's it. The ABAP FS extension spins up a local MCP server on your machine (default port 4847). You'll see a VS Code notification confirming it's running:
> ABAP MCP Server running on port 4847. External AI clients can connect to http://localhost:4847/mcp

Step 3.2 - Register the MCP Server with Claude Code
Now tell Claude Code where to find the ABAP tools. Run this in the VS Code integrated terminal:
This registers the local MCP server with Claude Code. The configuration is saved to your '.claude.json' file and persists across sessions - you only need to do this once.
Step 3.3 - Verify the Connection

 

open the terminal and run this command once the MCP server is added 
You should see:
Claude Code will confirm when the server is registered:
> abap-remote-fs has been added with HTTP transport at http://localhost:4847/mcp"*
The Complete Architecture
Claude Code can now autonomously:
- Search and read any ABAP object on the live SAP system
- write → syntax check → activate → unlock objects
- Create transport requests and assign objects
- Run ATC checks and read short dumps - all from a plain-English prompt
> Golden - In Auto mode, Claude will write, syntax check, and activate without pausing - that's the power of the agentic workflow. If you want to review before anything is activated, switch to Plan mode  shift+tab to cycle modes)  Claude will show you the full plan and wait for your approval. For production-adjacent systems, always run in Plan or Step mode.
Part 4 - Live Demo: One Prompt, One Real ABAP Program on SAP
Disclaimer: This is my first time setting up all this and explore, so i just started with a very simple scenario. Going forward will create RAP objects and other complex artifacts and see the efficiency.
Let me show you what all of this enables. Here's the exact prompt I used:
> Create a new ABAP executable program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in my ZVS_DEMO package. Fetch data from MARA for a user-specified Material Number (select-option) and display Material Number, Type, and Created By in an ALV grid. Write in ABAP 7.5 syntax.
One prompt. No templates. No copy-paste. Here's what Claude did.
Act 1 - Claude Plans Before It Codes
Claude didn't immediately start writing code. It:
1. Confirmed the target package ('ZVS_DEMO') exists on the system
2. Detected that the program creation triggered a **transport request dialog** — and recognised it was the likely source of a cancellation
3. Called the SAP ADT API via Bash to **list existing open transports**
4. Selected the correct transport and retried the creation
There was a little bit delay on my end to confirm the transport. and it gave me this message.
> Package confirmed. The cancellation is likely a transport request dialog. Let me check for existing transports, then retry.
This is the part that impresses ABAP developers most. Claude handled a real SAP workflow quirk .
Act 2 - The Program Appears on SAP
Claude then created the program on the live system. You can watch its autonomous todo list mid-execution:
- Create ABAP program ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01 in ZVS_DEMO package via MCP
- Get workspace URI for the new program
- Write ABAP source code into the program
- Activate the program and check diagnostics
'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' appeared in the VS Code Explorer sidebar - a real, registered ABAP program on the SAP system, not a local file.
You can see initially there was no program created in my package 
After few seconds, the program is created 

 

Act 3 - Full ABAP 7.5 Source Code Written
With the object created, Claude fetched the workspace URI and wrote the complete source code directly into the SAP program. What it generated:
No placeholder code. Real, working ABAP in the correct syntax release, referencing actual MARA field types from the live system.
Act 4 - Running the Program
After activation, there are two ways to run it:
Option A - Classic SAP GUI:Log in to SAP GUI →run the program → Execute
Option B - Embedded SAP Web GUI(what I used): VS Code can open the SAP Web GUI directly in a browser tab, embedded inside the IDE. No separate login or window switching required.
Enter selection criteria:
The selection screen appeared with the Matnr  field exactly as specified. I entered a range of 11to 21. The value help (F4) shows the available materials in the system
Material Number, Material Type, and Created By - exactly what was asked for. Live data from MARA. Report running.
 
Summary:
|Claude understands SAP context| It identified the transport dialog, queried existing transports via ADT, and handled a real SAP workflow quirk autonomously 
| It created a real SAP object| 'ZCL_DEMO_REPORT_01' exists on the live system, created via ADT REST API through MCP - not a file on disk 
| Production-quality ABAP 7.5 | TYPE declarations referencing actual MARA field types, correct naming conventions, modern SQL syntax 
| Minutes, not hours| The developer's job shifts to reviewing - not writing skeletons, hunting transport dialogs, or looking up field types 
| Fully iterative| Once created, you can ask Claude to add authority checks, error handling, ALV field catalogues, or additional filters - each iteration in seconds 
| You stay in control | Claude's todo list is transparent - you see every step it plans. You review the code. You decide what activates 
 
Where to Go from Here?
Here are five things to try once you have the setup working:
1. Understand inherited code - Explain the purpose and flow of report Z_*_REPORT. Include tables accessed, authority checks, deprecated patterns like SELECT .
2. Generate a CDS view - Create a CDS view hierarchy for Sales Order analysis: interface view joining VBAK+VBAP, projection view with Fiori Elements. UI annotations for a List Report.
3. Build a Travel Request POC -  RAP Business Object with CRUD, approve/reject action, draft handling, OData V4 binding, Fiori Elements UI.
4. Write ABAP Unit tests - Generate a unit test class 
5. Code review and modernization - Review this class for Clean ABAP violations, missing authority checks, SELECT * usage, and SELECT inside LOOP. Propose S/4-equivalent replacements.
 
The bottom line: The integration that used to require manual Node.js setup, JSON editing, and external package management now takes a single Ctrl+Shift+P toggle and one terminal command.
 
The ABAP Remote Filesystem team has done the hard work. Claude Code does the coding. You do the review.
Give it a try - and let me know in the comments what use case you tackle first.
 
A personal note  : This is my first time diving deeper into vibe coding and MCP concepts. I come from a classic ABAP background, and everything covered here -  from the VS Code setup to the MCP server to the live demo - I explored and built from scratch over the past few weeks. The scenario is intentionally simple. The goal was to get the fundamentals right, document them honestly, and share what works. There is a lot more ground to cover - RAP generation at scale, Clean Core extensibility patterns, ATC pre-checks via Claude Code, and deeper agentic workflows. I'll be going further in the posts ahead. If you're also just getting started with this, I hope this gives you a solid, working foundation to build on. And if you're already further ahead - I'd love to hear what you've learnt in the comments.
 
Note: All screenshots in this post are taken from a live S/4HANA development system. Code generated by Claude Code should always be reviewed and tested in a sandbox environment before activation in any productive system.

entry.title: Cutover control for SAP Cloud Integration: CSV driven bulk shutdown and wave based start of iFlows
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION

I am working on a large ECC to S/4HANA migration where the SAP Cloud Integration tenant stays the same .Existing iFlows are repointed to S/4HANA rather than rebuilt. The cutover therefore requires interfaces talking to ECC to be stopped before data migration, repointed, and brought back up in a controlled sequence. Crucially, only the iFlows touching the migrating SAP system need to stop; the same tenant runs third party and non-SAP interfaces that must keep running. So "undeploy everything" is not an option . What is needed is selective undeploy and deploy of a defined list and this is exactly what this tool does.

On SAP PI/PO this was a solved problem: PIMON let you filter, multi-select and stop/start channels centrally, with status visible in the same place. CPI has no equivalent . The web UI handles one artifact at a time, which in a cutover window is slow and error prone. The model also differs: PI stopped the channel while the scenario stayed in place; in CPI the lever is undeploying the artifact from the runtime — design untouched, but stopping means removing the deployed artifact.

REQUIREMENT


The case for a tool comes down to two numbers — time and error rate. At real cutover scale, say 250 iFlows in scope:

  1. Reduce time. Manually, each undeploy is filter, select, click Undeploy, confirm, verify — 30 to 45 seconds per iFlow on a responsive tenant. That is close to 3 hours of pure clicking for 250 iFlows, and with scope cross-checking, evidence capture and interruptions, realistically 4 to 6 hours, usually split across several people — which adds its own coordination risk of items falling between the splits. The tool executes the same 250 undeploys via the API in under 10 minutes.

  2. Reduce error rate. Human accuracy on a repetitive 250-item selection task over hours is not 100%. Even a 1–2% slip rate means 2 to 5 wrong outcomes: an in-scope iFlow missed and still pumping messages into ECC during data migration, or an out-of-scope one undeployed — taking down a live interface that had nothing to do with the cutover. Worst of all, manual misses are silent. With the tool, the scope is a reviewed CSV and the follow-up -status run is a complete, deterministic audit of all 250 — not a memory of what got clicked.

    This blog describes a command line tool I built to solve this .The setup is:

    CSV list of iFlows --> Java CLI tool --> CPI OData API (v1) --> CPI runtime

    During cutover we needed:

    1. A reviewed and signed off list of in-scope iFlows

    2. A status check before the shutdown window (everything STARTED)

    3. A bulk undeploy of exactly that list

    4. A status check after (everything gone from runtime

    5. Console output that can be pasted into the cutover log as evidence

Only the interfaces in scope should be touched. Other integrations on the tenant keep running.


THE TOOL

The tool is a single jar Java 21 application taking three arguments:

java -jar cpi-iflow-controller-1.1.0.jar <config-file> <iflows-csv> <-status|-undeploy|-deploy>

The iFlow list is a two column CSV — package and iFlow name:

Package,iFlow Name
PACKAGEMONTHLYEXTRACTINTERFACES,MONTHLY_TAX_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_BILLING_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_MASTER_DATA
PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS,OTC_FI_DATA


Note the Package column contains technical package Ids.

When the cutover task says "stop in-scope interfaces", the definition of in-scope is this file.

 

GOTCHA : Package name and Package Id are not the same thing


This one cost me a debugging session. I first populated the CSV from the Manage Integration Content monitor, where every deployed iFlow shows a Package field:

The first run failed for every iFlow with "iFlow not found in package" — even though the monitor showed all of them as Started:




The reason: the Package field in the monitor is the package display name, but the OData API keys on the technical package Id — and the two differ. The Id is auto-generated from the name with special characters (including underscores) stripped, so a package named PACKAGE_OTC_OBJECTS has the Id PACKAGEOTCOBJECTS:



The same applies to artifacts — an iFlow has a display name and a technical Id, and the API wants the Id. In my case the two matched (the monitor shows both, under Name and ID), but do not assume they do.

Where to find the technical Ids:

  • Open the package in Design — the Package ID is on the Overview tab and in the browser URL (.../shell/design/contentpackage/<Id>)

  • Or query the API directly:

curl -s -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \

"https://<tmn-host>/api/v1/IntegrationPackages?$select=Id,Name&$format=json"

With the technical Ids in the CSV, the same run succeeds:

One more useful behaviour: for -status the package column can be left empty. The runtime API only needs the iFlow Id, so the tool skips package resolution and queries IntegrationRuntimeArtifacts directly:

Rule of thumb: the CSV takes technical Ids, not the display names in the monitor. For status-only runs the package column is optional; for deploy it must be correct, because the design-time lookup depends on it.

CHECKING STATUS
